Project 01 · Mobile App · Civic Tech · Solo · BrachSpace

GRING — Waste Management App

Spring 2024 · BrachSpace · Individual Project

Overview

GRING is a mobile application connecting residents with garbage collection companies for more efficient, data-driven disposal. The core function relies on optimized service request matching to streamline pickup scheduling, with real-time status updates and educational modules for proper waste sorting.

Technical Highlights

Built using App Inventor with a backend data-driven matching algorithm. The workflow diagram above — built with Lucidchart — shows the full service request lifecycle: user submission → location verification → company matching → confirmation → pickup completion → educational tip delivery.

Why It Matters

In Yaoundé, Cameroon, informal waste collection is inconsistent and inaccessible. GRING addresses this gap by digitizing the request process, making reliable disposal available to any resident with a smartphone.

Stack & Tools

App InventorLucidchartMobile App DevSystems DesignCivic Tech
Project 02 · Mobile App · Team Lead · TiC Summit 2022

Toolman App — Tradespeople Platform

Summer 2022 · Tech Innovation Cameroon · Team: Tech Hawk Eyes

🔨
Toolman
Connecting skilled tradespeople with clients

Overview

Toolman is a mobile platform designed to provide market exposure and client connections for skilled tradespeople — carpenters, plumbers, electricians, and others — who lack digital visibility. Built and pitched at the 2nd edition of the TiC Summit Challenge 2022, which brought together secondary students across Cameroon through ideation, prototyping, and a business plan pitch to a national panel of judges.

My Role

As Team Leader and Front-End Developer for Team Hawk Eyes, I managed the front-end design and functional testing, coordinated team tasks under tight deadlines, prioritized UI/UX consistency across devices, and delivered the final pitch to national judges — refining my ability to communicate technical concepts to diverse stakeholders.

Stack & Tools

App InventorFront-End DesignIT Project ManagementPitchingTiC 2022
Project 06 · Arduino · Hardware · ECE 1100 · Discovery Project

Portable Magnetic Horn

For Personal Transport Safety · ECE 1100 · Georgia Tech

Overview

A self-contained electronic horn designed for bicycles, scooters, and personal transport devices. Modern mechanical bells lack consistency and audibility — this project addresses that gap with a battery-powered, toggle-triggered, Arduino-driven horn in a 3D-printed enclosure that magnetically clips onto any device.

Key Learnings

DC vs AC: Discovered why passive buzzers need AC/PWM — DC alone only produces a single click. PWM Frequency: Learned how Arduino's tone() function generates a square wave at exactly the right Hz for a horn sound. Iteration: Fixed a non-sounding first build by tracing component behavior step by step.

Components

9V BatteryPiezo BuzzerArduino UnoToggle SwitchNeodymium MagnetsBreadboard3D-Printed Enclosure

Georgia Tech · CS 1301 · Three Streamlit Applications

Multi-page Streamlit app with a personal portfolio page and a BuzzFeed-style fruit personality quiz. First full web application built in CS 1301.

Lab 02 — Data Collection & Visualization

Live CSV pipeline with a survey form, static bar chart, dynamic scatter plot filtered by slider, and aggregated line chart. Demonstrates end-to-end data flow from user input to visual output.

Lab 03 — AI-Powered Meal Discovery

TheMealDB API + Google Gemini LLM integration delivering a meal finder with cuisine distribution charts, a conversational chatbot, and an AI recipe analyzer with culinary insights.

About Me

Engineer by training.
Community-builder by choice.

My engineering journey began not in a formal lab, but in my community in Yaoundé, Cameroon — where observing systemic challenges in waste management and resource access led me to co-found BrachSpace, my social impact venture.

This foundation shapes how I approach every technical challenge with a dual lens: Is it technically sound, and does it solve a meaningful human problem? It's why I chose Georgia Tech's rigorous dual-degree path in Computer Engineering and Aerospace Engineering.

I believe the future of secure, autonomous systems lies at the intersection of two threads: Cybersecurity and Computer Hardware. The Cybersecurity thread addresses vulnerabilities; the Hardware thread designs security directly into physical infrastructure.

Beyond the code, my identity is defined by leadership and mentorship. I've guided young women aged 12–18 through Technovation and STEMin Africa, and completion of the Aspire Leaders Program solidified my approach to intentional, civic-purpose leadership.

The Journey

From Yaoundé
to Atlanta.

High school competitions in Cameroon. HCC. First day at Georgia Tech. These moments shaped who I am as an engineer and as a person.

"Every room I've walked into — from secondary school science fairs in Yaoundé to the engineering buildings at Georgia Tech — I carried the same belief: that technology should serve the people who need it most."

Career Roadmap

A roadmap to Trusted Computing.

My long-term goal is to become a Technical Lead or Architect in Trusted Computing — designing security directly into hardware infrastructure.

Aug 2025 – 2027
Foundation
3.5 GPA. Join YJSP Avionics for embedded systems and firmware hands-on experience.
2027 – 2028
Specialization
VIP team in IoT Security or Secure Hardware. Undergraduate research on Trusted Platform Modules.
Summer 2026
Industry
Co-op or internship in hardware verification, firmware testing, or hardware validation.
2028 – Dec 2029
Culmination
Senior Design in secure hardware/firmware. Finalize full-time or graduate school applications.
2033 – 2035
★ Vision
Technical Lead in Trusted Computing. Direct BrachSpace security strategy within 10 years of graduation.
Long-Term Vision
"Design security into the hardware itself — not as an afterthought, but as the foundation."
